clinical_history: This ECG with a wide QRS, recorded from a middle-age man, is most consistent with which ONE of the following diagnostic categories?
diagnosis: This challenging ECG shows sinus tachycardia at rest (about 100/min) with a uniformly long PR interval of at least 240 msec (first degree AV block). The low amplitude P waves are best seen in the magnified views. The wide QRS complexes show a right bundle branch block morphology (rSR´ in V1) with marked left axis deviation, consistent with left anterior fascicular block. Minuscule or absent inferior lead r waves raise the question of a previous inferior infarction (MI); the decreased lateral R waves also raise question of prior lateral MI. Relatively low amplitude QRS complexes are present. Of particular note are the T waves, which have a subtle symmetric, peaked appearance (either negative or positive, even though they are not of absolute high amplitude). This finding of relative T wave peaking raises the strong consideration of hyperkalemia. The intraventricular conduction delays can also be induced or worsened by hyperkalemia, as can AV block. This patient had an arterial K+ of about 6.9 mEq/L at this time, with a history of severe decompensated chronic renal failure and advanced coronary disease.
